---
title: "Understanding the Data Model Tables"
author: "Corey Sparks, PhD and Chip O'Connell, PhD"
subtitle: "Module 2: Notebook 3a"
toc: true
toc_depth: 3
toc_float:
  collapsed: no
  smooth_scroll: yes
toc_location: left
number-sections: true
format: 
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    df-print: paged
execute:
  eval: false
---



![](images/ada_sticker.png){fig-align="center"}

# Introduction
```{r setup, include=FALSE}
#HIDE THIS CHUNK FROM KNITTED OUTPUT
knitr::opts_chunk$set(include=TRUE, echo=TRUE, eval = FALSE,  warning = FALSE, fig.align = 'center')  #results=‘hide’) # needs to delete results=‘hide’
```
Welcome to the 

This notebook covers the details of the data model tables we have constructed for the training. 

Analyses involving administrative data often require:
-	Linking observations from multiple sources
-	Mediating differences in semantics
-	Mediating differences in grain (month versus quarter)
-	Mediating differences in cardinality and the potential to unintentionally exclude or overreport values
-	Facilitating intuitive and efficient processing and analysis of very large record sets
-	Mediating differences in names and relationships over time

This notebook will introduce and demonstrate some helpful techniques for linking administrative data while mediating the above issues.  The output of the notebook should provide a example, logic based framework that meets the needs of many projects and can be easily customized to include additional variables or characteristics.

The linked data asset documented in this notebook has already been completely created and loaded in the **tr_ihep_2025_pa database** as tables beginning with an "PA" prefix, with the final fact table titled **PA_FACT_Quarterly_Observation_new**.  This notebook will not create or load duplicative copies of the linked dataset but rather cover the techniques used to construct and load the model and hopefully serve as a resource to use when building future linked data sets. 


## The Purpose of this Notebook

You will now have the opportunity to apply the skills covered thus far to restricted use PASSHE data. With your team, you will carry out a detailed analysis of this data and prepare a final project showcasing your results.

These workbooks are here to help you along in this project development by showcasing how to apply the techniques discussed in class to the PASSHE data. Part of this notebook will be technical - providing basic code snippets your team can **modify** to begin parsing through the data. As always, however, there will also be an applied data literacy component of these workbooks, and it should help you develop a better understanding of the structure and use of the underlying data even if you never writer a line of code.

# Technical Setup

Code chunks in this notebook will be provided in both SQL and R, so follow the setup sections below if you are interested in establishing your own SQL and R environments for running the code.

Typically, throughout these workbooks, we use SQL for the majority of data exploration and creation of the analytic frame, and then read that analytic frame into R for the descriptive analysis and visualization. You will see options for writing SQL code in R, but not vice versa. We discuss this approach in a later section.

::: {.callout collapse="true"}
## SQL Setup

For working with the database directly using SQL, the easiest way is to still copy the SQL commands from this notebook (available within the `SQL Query` tab views) into a script in DBeaver. As a reminder, the steps to do so are as follows:

1.  Open DBeaver, located on the ADRF Desktop. The icon looks like this

![](images/dbeaver.png)

2.  Establish your connection to the database by logging in. To do this, double-click `Redshift11_projects` on the left hand side, and it will ask for your username and password. Your username is `adrf\` followed by the name of your U: drive folder - for example, `adrf\JOhn.Doe.T00113`. Your password is the same password that you use to log into the ADRF.

After you successfully establish your connection, you should see a green check next to the database name, like so:

![](images/success.png)

3.  In the top menu bar, click **SQL Editor** then **New SQL Script**:

![](images/new_script.png)

4.  To test if your connection is working, try pasting the following chunk of code into your script:

```{sql, eval=FALSE}
SELECT *
FROM ds_pa_sshe.enrollment
LIMIT 5;
```

Then run it by clicking the run button next to the script, or by pressing CTRL + ENTER.

5.  You should then be able to see the query output in the box below the code.
:::

::: {.callout collapse="true"}
## R Setup

#### Install Packages {.unnumbered}

Because we have left the world of the Foundations Module and entered a new workspace, we need to re-install packages that are not native to the base R environment. **We only need to do this once - this workflow for programming in R is identical to that outside the ADRF.**

::: callout-note
In the ADRF, you are limited to installing packages available on the Comprehensive R Archive Networks, commonly referred to as CRAN. CRAN is the primary centralized repository for R packages. Packages must meet certain requirements before they can become available on CRAN. Outside the ADRF, you can install packages available on other repositories, such as those accessible on Github repositories.
:::

To install a package, in the console in R studio, type the following:

```{r, eval=FALSE}
install.packages('INSERT_PACKAGE_NAME')
```

For example, to install the package `RJDBC` (for establishing our connection to the database), you should type:

```{r, eval=FALSE}
install.packages("RJDBC")
```

If you run this, you should see a set of messages in your console similar to the below:

![](images/RJDBC_install.png)

After running this, `RJDBC` will be installed for you within your ADRF workspace. You will not need to reinstall it each time you log back into the workspace.

There are two simple ways to identify if a package has already been installed in your environment:

1.  You can run `library(INSERT_PACKAGE_NAME)`, and if it returns an error like the following, it has not been installed properly:

![](images/error.png)

2.  You can manually check in the bottom right pane inside R Studio by selecting the Packages tab and seeing if the package is available. Packages should be sorted alphabetically and those with a check beside them have not just already been installed, but also loaded into the environment for use:

![](images/package_pane.png)

**To install all of the packages you will need for this notebook, please run the following:**

```{r, eval=FALSE}
install.packages(RJDBC)
install.packages(tidyverse)
install.packages(dbplyr)
```

Additionally, in order to establish our database connection in R, please install the following custom package that has already been uploaded into the P: drive of this workspace:

```{r, eval=FALSE}
install.packages("P:/tr-ihep-2025-pa/R_packages/ColeridgeInitiative_0.1.2.zip")
```

#### Load Libraries {.unnumbered}

After installing these packages, we next need to load them to make them available in our R environment. This is identical to the procedure we followed in the Foundations Module

```{r}
library(ColeridgeInitiative)
library(tidyverse)
library(dbplyr)
```

#### Establish Database Connection {.unnumbered}

To load data from the Redshift server into R, we need to first set up a connection to the database. The following command will prompt you for password and establish the connection:

```{r}
con <- adrf_redshift(usertype = "training")
```
:::

# Data model rationale

To facilitate easy and performant analysis of very large record sets (quarterly wages, for example), we will be formatting the data in a dimensional model.  This type of model:
-	Facilitates efficient and intuitive storage, processing, and analysis of very large, linked data sets
-	Facilitates slicing, dicing, and drilling for exploratory data analysis
-	Provides excellent performance for dashboards and visualizations

The modeling process involves conjugating the data into events and observations (verbs/facts) and the entities and attributes with which they are associated and by which they are analyzed (nouns/dimensions) (Kimball and Ross, 2019).

The SQL scripts for the actual creation of the dimensional model used in this notebook are in the supplemental folder in a subfolder titled `"P:\tr-ihep-2025-pa\Team Leads Folder\data_model_scripts"` They include statements to create the tables and notes on the logic used to create the measures captured by the different tables.  

Physical diagrams and metadata for this dimensional model have been added to the References page of the class website.

You will not need to create additional tables, but you may wish to review the SQL as a reference for creating dimensional models for future projects or adapting the code to create your own data model for your class project.

### Merged Dimensions

The modeling process starts with identifying the dimensions that describe the observations of interest and by which they will be analyzed. These will be combined into dimension entities (tables) that can merge attributes (columns) from multiple data sources. Some of the advantages of using merged dimensions include:

-	Mediating differences in semantics
-	Facilitating easy hierarchy navigation
-	Allowing for easy expansion with additional attributes without disrupting the much larger table of observations
-	Referencing data dimensions that have an external registration authority for interoperability across departments, states, and sectors (such as CIP codes)


### Time Dimension

A special type of dimension that is helpful for longitudinal analysis is a time dimension.  This is a dimension that stores all possible values for a period of time (day, week, quarter, month, year) across a long period and allows for easy cross-referencing across time periods such as day to quarter, academic year, financial aid year, etc.

Using an incrementing integer identifier as the primary key for time dimensions is particularly useful for longitudinal analysis as it facilitates comparison across periods through simple arithmetic. For example, in order to find outcomes for the 4th quarter following quarter of degree completion t, you simply need to look up t+4. The tricky part is identifying a consistent grain, especially when they may overlap depending on the dataset, which we see in the academic tables (semesters) and wage records (quarters). Due to the focus on completer cohorts within the PVF, we aligned the academic term with its *beginning* quarter - for example, the spring semester lines up with quarter 3.

By encoding all dates at a consistent grain (quarter) and representation (incrementing integer), it makes it easy to conduct analyses based on relative longitudinal outcomes (4 quarters past exit for all degree completions between 2015 and 2018) in additional to absolute longitudinal outcomes (2015 Q3 employment for 2015 Q1 degree completers).  This is especially helpful when smaller data sets limit the cohort size for absolute cohort outcomes.

To construct the time dimension, we set up a loop that increments between a starting and ending period and derives various time period representations and relationships needed for our analysis.

```{sql, eval=FALSE}
WITH date_series AS(
SELECT DATEADD(MONTH, n * 3, DATE '2001-01-01') AS date
FROM (
  SELECT ROW_NUMBER() OVER () - 1 AS n
  FROM ds_pa_sshe.completions c -- use any table with at least ~1000 rows
  LIMIT 1000
) AS seq
WHERE DATEADD(MONTH, n * 3, DATE '2001-01-01') <= DATE '2025-12-31'
),
calendar_quarters AS (
    SELECT
        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY ds.date) AS quarter_id,
        ds.date AS start_date,
        DATEADD(MONTH, 3, ds.date) - INTERVAL '1 day' AS end_date,
        EXTRACT(YEAR FROM ds.date) AS calendar_year,
        EXTRACT(QUARTER FROM ds.date) AS calendar_quarter,
        TO_CHAR(EXTRACT(YEAR FROM ds.date), '9999') || ' Q' || EXTRACT(QUARTER FROM ds.date) AS quarter_label,
        EXTRACT(MONTH FROM ds.date) AS calendar_month_start,
        EXTRACT(MONTH FROM DATEADD(MONTH, 3, ds.date) - INTERVAL '1 day') AS calendar_month_end
    FROM date_series ds
),
valid_academic_periods AS (
    SELECT
        acad_pd_code,
        acad_term,
        acad_pd_desc,
        TRY_CAST(start_dt AS DATE) AS start_dt,
        TRY_CAST(end_dt AS DATE) AS end_dt
    FROM tr_ihep_2025_pa.academic_period_table
    WHERE TRY_CAST(start_dt AS DATE) IS NOT NULL
      AND TRY_CAST(end_dt AS DATE) IS NOT NULL
),
quarter_acad_period AS (
    SELECT
        cq.*,
        ap.acad_pd_code,
        ap.acad_term,
        ap.acad_pd_desc,
        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY cq.quarter_id ORDER BY
            LEAST(ap.end_dt, cq.end_date)::date - GREATEST(ap.start_dt, cq.start_date)::date DESC
        ) AS rn
    FROM calendar_quarters cq
    LEFT JOIN valid_academic_periods ap
        ON ap.end_dt >= cq.start_date AND ap.start_dt <= cq.end_date
)
SELECT
    quarter_id,
    start_date,
    end_date,
    quarter_label,
    calendar_year,
    calendar_quarter,
    calendar_month_start,
    calendar_month_end,

    -- Academic Term Name
    CASE calendar_quarter
        WHEN 1 THEN 'Winter'
        WHEN 2 THEN 'Spring'
        WHEN 3 THEN 'Summer'
        ELSE 'Fall'
    END AS academic_term,

    -- Academic Year (assuming AY starts in Fall)
    CASE WHEN calendar_quarter >= 3 THEN calendar_year ELSE calendar_year - 1 END AS academic_year,

    -- Financial Aid Year (often aligns similarly)
    CASE WHEN calendar_quarter = 4 THEN calendar_year ELSE calendar_year - 1 END AS financial_aid_year,

    -- Quarter Index
    calendar_year * 4 + (calendar_quarter - 1) AS quarter_index,

    -- Academic Period fields
    acad_pd_code,
    acad_term AS acad_term_detail,
    acad_pd_desc
FROM quarter_acad_period
WHERE rn = 1 -- take the academic period overlapping most days
ORDER BY start_date;


```


## Primary data generation
Unlike reference data that is consistent across states (CIP, NAICS for industry codes), primary data refer to the unique collection of persons, employers, or households served by each state.  A state can have many different references to the same real-world entity, and the process followed here is the processing of assembling a set that has one member (record) for each unique instance of an entity in the real world.  

This primary record can merge attributes from multiple sources, resulting in a golden record with a higher completeness than is available in individual sources. When multiple references to the same entity have different values, those differences are resolved through a process called survivorship in which decisions are made about which value to keep (most recent, most frequent, highest quality source, etc.).

In our data, we already have a primary person table developed as part of this training (conveniently titled **pa_mdim_person**) that contains any individuals from any data source in the PASSHE Data System. The table already includes some time invariant variables, such as race/ethnicity and gender, date of birth and Pell grant status, and contains one record per person. Due to our intention of applying the PVF to this data, we will leverage some data from this table, as well as some other time invariant variables. Since the PVF often assigns cohorts to indviduals by their highest, most recent degree, we will add this information to our time-invariant, person table. Additionally, we will limit our person table to those who appear in the enrollments data.

```{sql, eval=FALSE}
-- person mastering
with ssn_key as (
    select ssn, dense_rank() over (order by ssn) as person_key
    from (
        select distinct ssn
        from ds_pa_sshe.enrollment
    ) a
),

gender_tab as (
    select ssn, sex_code, count(*) as cnt,
           row_number() over (partition by ssn order by count(*) desc,
                              case when sex_code = 'F' then 1
                                   when sex_code = 'M' then 2
                                   else 3 end) as rn
    from ds_pa_sshe.enrollment e2
    where sex_code is not null
    group by ssn, sex_code
),

gender_tab2 as (
    select * 
    from gender_tab
    where rn = 1
),

eth_counts as (
  select
    ssn,
    race_ethnicity,
    count(*) as cnt
  from ds_pa_sshe.enrollment
  where race_ethnicity is not null
  group by ssn, race_ethnicity
),
eth_ranked as (
  select
    ssn,
    race_ethnicity,
    row_number() over (
      partition by ssn
      order by 
        case 
          when lower(race_ethnicity) in ('unknown', 'missing', 'not reported', '') then 2
          else 1
        end,
        cnt desc
    ) as rn
  from eth_counts
),
eth as (
  select ssn, race_ethnicity
  from eth_ranked
  where rn = 1
)
,
dob_tab as (
    select e2.ssn, e2.birthday, count(*) as cnt,
           row_number() over (partition by ssn order by count(*) desc, birthday) as rn
    from ds_pa_sshe.enrollment e2
    where birthday > '1900-01-01'
    group by ssn, birthday
),

dob_tab2 as (
    select *
    from dob_tab
    where rn = 1
),

pell as (
    select 
        fa.ssn, 
        case when max(pell) > 0 then 1 else 0 end as pellrecipient
    from ds_pa_sshe.financial_aid fa
    group by fa.ssn
)
select
    --sk.person_key,
    cl.ssn,
    cl.birthday,
    c2.race_ethnicity,
    c3.sex_code,
    coalesce(p.pellrecipient, 0) as pellrecipient
   -- case when p.pellrecipient is null then 0 else p.pellrecipient end as pellstatus
from dob_tab2 cl
left join eth c2 on cl.ssn = c2.ssn
left join gender_tab2 c3 on cl.ssn = c3.ssn
--left join ssn_key sk on cl.ssn = sk.ssn
left join pell p on cl.ssn = p.ssn
group by 1,2,3,4,5;


```



## Fact Tables
The fact table stores the actual observations (facts) of interest.  Since this table often contains large numbers of records, it will ideally be comprised of a small number of bytes per row and primarily consist of indexed foreign keys to dimension tables and observation-specific measures.  This allows for storage of large records sets with low storage cost and extremely high query performance (extremely helpful for supporting dashboards).

In this example, the fact table is at the grain of one row per person per quarter.  We will create a record for every quarter between the first and last **quarter_id** values generated in the time dimension table, regardless of enrollment status in a given quarter.  These missing observation quarters are materialized because non-enrollment may be just as interesting for some analyses and longitudinal analysis benefits from consistent representation across time periods of consistent grain.

Some of our cohort members have observations for multiple enrollments or completions in a single quarter (or term).  Since our unit of analysis is the person, not the person-enrollment/completion combination, we need to resolve these one-to-many relationships into a single observation while retaining the information pertinent to analysis.  In this example, the primary enrollment and completion per quarter, along with the associated information, were identified and recorded based on the institution with the most amount of credit bearing hours or highest degree, respectively. In order to minimize loss of potentially relevant information, the total enrollments and completions is also included on each observation.

We also matched financial aid information on the primary institution of enrollment - recall that financial aid is recorded yearly, so you may encounter repeat values, and be careful about summing them up within a given financial aid academic year. In our example, there may be multiple records for an individual in a year, even when matching on institution, so the record with the highest financial aid information was selected for the surviving financial aid values. The optimal survivorship strategy may vary based on the intended audience and purpose.

> This query is looking at large volumes of data and will run for several minutes if not filtered for specific observations.

```{sql, eval=FALSE}
with id as (
	select distinct pmp.ssn 
	from tr_ihep_2025_pa.pa_mdim_person pmp 
	limit 100 -- to shorten run time
),
--find all quarter_id values
q as (
	select distinct quarter_id
	from tr_ihep_2025_pa.pa_dim_quarterly_academic_periods  prq 
),
--join them to get all combinations of ssn and quarter_id
tot as (
	select id.ssn, q.quarter_id 
	from id 
	cross join q 
),
--join quarter_id to the enrollments data
enr_quarter as (
	select e.*, q.Quarter_ID, q.academic_term , q.academic_year 
	from ds_pa_sshe.enrollment e
	join tr_ihep_2025_pa.pa_dim_quarterly_academic_periods  q 
	on q.acad_pd_code = e.academic_period_code::numeric
),
-- find enrollment status
status as (
		select distinct tot.ssn,
	tot.quarter_id,
	case when enr.ssn is null then 'N' else 'Y' end as Enrollment_Status --if they show up in the enrollment quarter in a given value of
	from tot 
	left join enr_quarter enr --left join to keep all values of ssn and quarter_id
	on tot.ssn = enr.ssn and tot.quarter_id = enr.quarter_id 
),
--find number of total enrollments per quarter
enroll_per_q as (
	select status.ssn, status.quarter_id, count(distinct(e.ipeds_unitid)) as num_enrollments
	from status 
	left join enr_quarter e on (e.ssn = status.ssn and e.quarter_id = status.quarter_id)
	group by status.ssn, status.quarter_id
),
rank_inst as (
	select ke.ssn, ke.ipeds_unitid, krq.Financial_Aid_Year,
			row_number() over (
			partition by ke.ssn, krq.Financial_Aid_Year order by ke.ssn, krq.Financial_Aid_Year, sum(ke.credits_attempted_for_term) desc
		) as num 
	from ds_pa_sshe.enrollment ke 
	join tr_ihep_2025_pa.pa_dim_quarterly_academic_periods  krq 
	on krq.acad_pd_code = ke.academic_period_code::numeric 
	group by ke.ssn, ke.ipeds_unitid, krq.Financial_Aid_Year
),
dom_inst as (
	select ssn, ipeds_unitid, financial_aid_year 
	from rank_inst 
	where num = 1
),
dom_inst_info as (
	select dom_inst.*, krq2.quarter_id,
		ke2.credits_attempted_for_term , 
		ke2.credits_completed_for_term ,
		ke2.is_pa_resident_for_billing ,
		ke2.ug_gr ,
		ke2.fulltime_parttime ,
		ke2.is_first_time ,
		ke2.major1_cip6_code ,
		ke2.award_level ,
		row_number() over (
			partition by ke2.ssn, ke2.ipeds_unitid, krq2.quarter_id order by ke2.ssn, ke2.ipeds_unitid, krq2.Quarter_ID, ke2.credits_attempted_for_term  desc
		) as num 
	from dom_inst 
	join ds_pa_sshe.enrollment ke2 on 
		(dom_inst.ipeds_unitid = ke2.ipeds_unitid and dom_inst.ssn = ke2.ssn)
	join tr_ihep_2025_pa.pa_dim_quarterly_academic_periods krq2 on (krq2.Financial_Aid_Year = dom_inst.financial_aid_year and 
		krq2.acad_pd_code = ke2.academic_period_code::numeric )
		
),
dom_inst_info_ranked as (
select * 
from dom_inst_info 
where num = 1
),

--match to financial aid information, eventually join only on the primary institution information
fin_aid as (	
	select 
	distinct 
	kfa.ssn,
	kfa.academic_year , 
	max(kfa.total_gift_aid ) as totalgrantsallsources, --in case duplication on quarter_id and ipeds_unitid for grants with different values
	max(kfa.total_fed_loans) as totalloansallsources, --in case duplication on quarter_id and ipeds_unitid for loans with different values
	max(kfa.total_fed_workstudy) as totalworkallsources, --in case duplication on quarter_id and ipeds_unitid for work-study with different values
	max(kfa.pell) as pell_grants, --in case duplication on quarter_id and ipeds_unitid for pell grants with different values
	max(kfa.total_price) as totalprice,
	kfa.ipeds_unitid,
	krq.quarter_id
	from ds_pa_sshe.financial_aid kfa 
	join tr_ihep_2025_pa.pa_dim_quarterly_academic_periods krq on krq.academic_year = substring(kfa.academic_year from 1 for 4)::numeric 
	group by ssn, kfa.academic_year, ipeds_unitid, quarter_id
),
comp_quarter as (
	select kd.*, q.Quarter_ID, q.academic_year, q.academic_term
	from ds_pa_sshe.completions kd--  ds_ky_kystats.kpeds_degree kd 
	join tr_ihep_2025_pa.pa_dim_quarterly_academic_periods  q 
	on q.acad_pd_code = kd.academic_period_code 
	
),
--rank primary completion if multiple completions in a given quarter_id
comp_ranked as (
--if they show up in the completion quarter in a given value of quarter_id then they completed, otherwise not
	select tot.ssn, tot.quarter_id,
	case when comp.ssn is null then 'N' else 'Y' end as completion_status,
	comp.award_level, --  degreerank,
	--comp.kpeds_degreelevelshortdesc,
	comp.major1_cip6_code,
	comp.ipeds_unitid,
	row_number() over (
		partition by tot.ssn, tot.quarter_id order by tot.ssn, tot.quarter_id, comp.award_level
	) as num 
	from tot 
	left join comp_quarter comp --left join to keep all values of ssn and quarter_id
	on tot.ssn = comp.ssn and tot.quarter_id = comp.quarter_id 
),
--identify primary completion per quarter_id
first_comp as (
	select ssn,
	quarter_id, 
	completion_status,
	--degreerank,
	award_level,
	major1_cip6_code,
	ipeds_unitid
	from comp_ranked 
	where num = 1
),
--find amount of completions per quarter_id
comp_per_q as (
	select ssn,
	quarter_id,
	case when completion_status = 'N' then 0 else max(num) end as num_completions
	from comp_ranked 
	group by ssn, quarter_id, completion_status
),
--THIS IS WHERE WE ADDED IN THE WAGE RECORDS CTES
Wage_Rank AS (
	SELECT
	P.ssn,
	Q.Quarter_ID,
	ROW_NUMBER() OVER(PARTITION BY P.ssn, Q.Quarter_ID ORDER BY W.wages_real DESC) AS RANK,
	W.wages_real, --,
	W.employer_id ,
	w.naics_level6_code 
	FROM 
	ds_pa_dli.wage_records W
	JOIN tr_ihep_2025_pa.pa_mdim_person P ON (W.ssn = P.ssn)
	JOIN tr_ihep_2025_pa.pa_dim_quarterly_academic_periods  Q ON ((W.yr = Q.Calendar_Year) AND (W.qtr = Q.Calendar_Quarter))
),
Primary_Employer_Wage AS (
	SELECT
	WR.ssn,
	WR.Quarter_ID,
	WR.wages_real AS Primary_Employer_Wages,
	WR.employer_id as Primary_Employer_ID, 
	wr.naics_level6_code
	FROM
	Wage_Rank WR
	WHERE
	WR.RANK=1
),
All_Employer_Wage AS (
	SELECT 
	WR.ssn,
	WR.Quarter_ID,
	COUNT(WR.employer_id) AS Employer_Count,
	SUM(WR.wages_real) AS Total_Wages
	FROM Wage_Rank WR
	GROUP BY
	WR.ssn,
	WR.Quarter_ID
)
select 
tot.ssn, 
tot.quarter_id, 
status.enrollment_status,
d.ipeds_unitid as enrolled_primary_institution,
d.credits_attempted_for_term as enrolled_primary_hours_attempted,-- kpeds_creditbearinghours ,
d.credits_completed_for_term as enrolled_primary_hours_completed,
d.fulltime_parttime as enrolled_primary_full_time_ind,
d.is_first_time as enrolled_first_time,
d.major1_cip6_code as enrolled_primary_major,
d.award_level as enrolled_primary_degree_sought,
d.is_pa_resident_for_billing as enrolled_primary_residency,
enroll_per_q.num_enrollments as number_enrollments,
fin_aid.totalprice as primary_institution_total_price,
fin_aid.totalgrantsallsources as primary_institution_grant_aid,
fin_aid.totalloansallsources as primary_institution_loan_aid,
fin_aid.totalworkallsources as primary_institution_work_aid,
fin_aid.pell_grants as primary_institution_pell,
first_comp.completion_status as completion_status,
first_comp.award_level as completed_primary_degree,
 substring(first_comp.major1_cip6_code from 1 for 6) as completed_primary_major,
first_comp.ipeds_unitid as completed_primary_institution,
comp_per_q.num_completions as number_completions,
CASE WHEN PEW.ssn IS NULL THEN 'N' ELSE 'Y' END AS Employed,
PEW.Primary_Employer_ID,
PEW.Primary_Employer_Wages,
PEW.naics_level6_code,
AEW.Total_Wages,
AEW.Employer_Count
from tot 
join status on (tot.ssn = status.ssn and tot.quarter_id = status.quarter_id)
left join dom_inst_info_ranked d on (tot.ssn = d.ssn and tot.quarter_id = d.quarter_id)
join enroll_per_q on (tot.ssn = enroll_per_q.ssn and tot.quarter_id = enroll_per_q.quarter_id)
--join on primary enrolled institution for financial aid info, left join to preserve all values of ssn and quarter_id
left join fin_aid on (tot.ssn = fin_aid.ssn and tot.quarter_id = fin_aid.quarter_id and d.ipeds_unitid = fin_aid.ipeds_unitid)
join first_comp on (tot.ssn = first_comp.ssn and tot.quarter_id = first_comp.quarter_id)
join comp_per_q on (tot.ssn = comp_per_q.ssn and tot.quarter_id = comp_per_q.quarter_id)
LEFT JOIN Primary_Employer_Wage PEW ON (PEW.ssn=tot.ssn) AND (PEW.Quarter_ID=tot.Quarter_ID) 
LEFT JOIN All_Employer_Wage AEW ON (AEW.ssn=tot.ssn) AND (AEW.Quarter_ID=tot.Quarter_ID) 
order by tot.ssn, tot.quarter_id
```



### Cost fact table
A similar logic is used to create our second fact table, which measures the total cost of attendance, measured for each person, institution, and award level, including tuition, fees, room and board.

Because some students are not present in the financial aid table, we impute the cost of attendance using the `tuition_and_fees_lookup` table for a given year, institution and residency status. 

```{sql, eval=FALSE}
WITH student_first_year AS (
  SELECT
    ssn,
    MIN(SUBSTRING(academic_year, 1, 4)::NUMERIC) AS first_year
  FROM ds_pa_sshe.enrollment
  WHERE is_first_time = 'Y'
  GROUP BY ssn
),

-- Step 2: Max transfer credits per student
max_transfer_credits AS (
  SELECT
    ssn,
    MAX(credits_transferred) AS max_credits_transferred
  FROM ds_pa_sshe.enrollment
  WHERE is_transfer = 'Y'
  GROUP BY ssn
),

-- Step 3: Base data with net cost, credit totals, and transfer flag
base_data AS (
  SELECT
    e.ssn,
    e.ipeds_unitid,
    e.ug_gr,
    e.is_pa_resident_for_billing,
    e.is_transfer,
    COUNT(1) AS n,
       SUM(e.credits_completed_for_term) AS credits, -- total credits in year
  avg(fa.total_price) - avg(fa.total_gift_aid) as net_cost ,
  avg(fa.tuition)- avg(fa.total_gift_aid ) as net_cost_noroom,
  avg(fa.total_fed_loans) as total_loans,

    -- Flag for actual zero net cost
    CASE
      WHEN SUM(fa.total_price) - SUM(fa.total_gift_aid) <= 0 THEN TRUE
      ELSE FALSE
    END AS is_actual_zero_net_cost,

    SUBSTRING(e.academic_year, 1, 4)::NUMERIC AS yr,

    -- Yearly sum, preserving NULL if any part is NULL
    (SUM(fa.total_price) - SUM(fa.total_gift_aid)) / NULLIF(COUNT(1), 0) AS yearly_sum

  FROM ds_pa_sshe.enrollment e
  LEFT JOIN ds_pa_sshe.financial_aid fa
    ON e.ssn = fa.ssn
   AND e.academic_year = fa.academic_year
   AND e.ug_gr = fa.ug_gr
  WHERE SUBSTRING(e.academic_year, 1, 4)::NUMERIC >= 2009
  GROUP BY
    e.ssn, e.ipeds_unitid, e.ug_gr, e.is_pa_resident_for_billing, e.is_transfer, SUBSTRING(e.academic_year, 1, 4)
),

-- Step 4: Add first-year indicator and max transfer credits
enriched_data AS (
  SELECT
    b.*,
    mt.max_credits_transferred,
    CASE WHEN b.yr = f.first_year THEN TRUE ELSE FALSE END AS is_first_year
  FROM base_data b
  LEFT JOIN student_first_year f ON b.ssn = f.ssn
  LEFT JOIN max_transfer_credits mt ON b.ssn = mt.ssn
),

-- Step 5: Add cost estimates from lookup table, and compute total_cost
enriched_with_sumcost AS (
  SELECT 
    enriched_data.*,

    -- Lookup-based imputation for cost
    CASE 
      WHEN credits <= 24 THEN 
        COALESCE(tafl.tuition_per_credit, 0) * COALESCE(credits, 0) 
        + COALESCE(tafl.fees_required_parttime, 0)
      ELSE 
        COALESCE(tafl.tuition_fulltime, 0) 
        + COALESCE(tafl.fees_required_fulltime, 0) 
        + COALESCE(tafl.room, 0) 
        + COALESCE(tafl.board, 0)
    END AS sumcost,
    -- Transfer tuition cost only in first year using max credits
    CASE 
      WHEN is_transfer = 'Y'  THEN COALESCE(max_credits_transferred, 0) * COALESCE(tafl.tuition_per_credit, 0)
      ELSE 0
    END AS transfer_tuition_cost,

    -- Total cost = sumcost + one-time transfer credit cost in first year if applicable
    (CASE 
        WHEN credits <= 24 THEN 
          COALESCE(tafl.tuition_per_credit, 0) * COALESCE(credits, 0) 
          + COALESCE(tafl.fees_required_parttime, 0)
        ELSE 
          COALESCE(tafl.tuition_fulltime, 0) 
          + COALESCE(tafl.fees_required_fulltime, 0) 
          + COALESCE(tafl.room, 0) 
          + COALESCE(tafl.board, 0)
     END
     + CASE 
         WHEN is_transfer = 'Y'  THEN COALESCE(max_credits_transferred, 0) * COALESCE(tafl.tuition_per_credit, 0)
         ELSE 0
       END) AS total_cost,
 (CASE 
        WHEN credits <= 30 THEN 
          COALESCE(tafl.tuition_per_credit, 0) * COALESCE(credits, 0) 
          + COALESCE(tafl.fees_required_parttime, 0)
        ELSE 
          COALESCE(tafl.tuition_fulltime, 0) 
          + COALESCE(tafl.fees_required_fulltime, 0) 
     END
     + CASE 
         WHEN is_transfer = 'Y' AND is_first_year THEN COALESCE(max_credits_transferred, 0) * COALESCE(tafl.tuition_per_credit, 0)
         ELSE 0
       END) AS total_cost_noroom
  FROM enriched_data
  LEFT JOIN ds_pa_sshe.tuition_and_fees_lookup tafl
    ON enriched_data.ipeds_unitid = tafl.ipeds_unitid
   AND enriched_data.yr = tafl.ipeds_year
   AND enriched_data.ug_gr = tafl.ug_gr
   AND enriched_data.is_pa_resident_for_billing = tafl.is_pa_resident_for_billing
),

-- Step 6: Replace yearly_sum with imputed value if missing
final_with_adjusted_sum AS (
  SELECT 
    *,
    CASE 
      WHEN yearly_sum = 0 OR yearly_sum IS NULL THEN sumcost
      ELSE yearly_sum
    END AS adjusted_yearly_sum
  FROM enriched_with_sumcost
),

-- Step 7: Compute cumulative cost
final_with_running_sum AS (
  SELECT 
    *,
    SUM(total_cost) OVER (
      PARTITION BY ssn, ipeds_unitid, ug_gr, is_pa_resident_for_billing
      ORDER BY yr
      ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
    ) AS updated_running_sum,
    SUM(total_loans) OVER (
      PARTITION BY ssn, ipeds_unitid, ug_gr, is_pa_resident_for_billing
      ORDER BY yr
      ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
    ) AS updated_running_loans,
    SUM(total_cost_noroom) OVER (
      PARTITION BY ssn, ipeds_unitid, ug_gr, is_pa_resident_for_billing
      ORDER BY yr
      ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
    ) AS updated_running_sum_noroom
  FROM final_with_adjusted_sum
),

-- Final Output: One row per student-institution-program with max cost
ranked_rows as (SELECT *
FROM (
  SELECT *,
         ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
           PARTITION BY ssn, ipeds_unitid, ug_gr
           ORDER BY updated_running_sum DESC
         ) AS rn
  FROM final_with_running_sum
) ranked
WHERE rn = 1
ORDER BY ssn, ipeds_unitid, ug_gr)
SELECT
  ssn,
  ipeds_unitid,
  ug_gr,
  is_pa_resident_for_billing,
  is_transfer,
  transfer_tuition_cost,
  case when updated_running_sum <0 then 0 else updated_running_sum end AS total_cost, --floor total cost at 0
  case when updated_running_sum_noroom <0 then 0 else updated_running_sum_noroom end AS total_cost_noroom,
  updated_running_loans as total_loans,
  yr AS last_year_included,
  --is_first_year,
  is_actual_zero_net_cost
FROM ranked_rows
ORDER BY ssn, ipeds_unitid, ug_gr;

```

### Creating your own cohort

Since we performed the cohort filtering in R, we can save the final cohort definition as a csv file or a table in the `tr_ihep_2025_pa` schema. We can also save the file as a permanent table in R, which will allow us to leverage SQL's join functionality to link the cohort to large tables that are unable to be read into R due to memory constraints. The following code blocks demonstrate how to convert an R data frame to a permanent table by creating the table **nb_cohort_2015** in the VDC training workspace database **tr_ihep_2025_pa** from the data model table.

> Note: Saving your team's cohort as a permanent table is highly recommended. When doing so, please save your table with the prefix team# (ex. team1_cohort) to differentiate between tables for each project.

```{sql, eval=FALSE}
drop table if exists tr_ihep_2025_pa.nb_cohort_2015;
create table tr_ihep_2025_pa.nb_cohort_2015 as 
SELECT q01.ssn, q01.quarter_id as nb_quarter, 
q01.enrolled_primary_institution, 
q01.enrolled_primary_major_level,
q01.enrolled_primary_major

FROM (
  SELECT
    pa_fact_quarterly_observation_new.*,
    MIN(quarter_id) OVER (PARTITION BY ssn) AS min_qtr
  FROM tr_ihep_2025_pa.pa_fact_quarterly_observation_new
  WHERE (enrolled_primary_major_level = 'UG' AND enrollment_status = 'Y')
) q01
WHERE (quarter_id = min_qtr) AND (quarter_id = 60.0); -- modify your quarter id  to match the time you want

grant SELECT, UPDATE, DELETE, INSERT ON TABLE tr_ihep_2025_pa.nb_cohort_2015  to  group db_t00137_rw;
```
